subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text:
STUDIUM
 
CMLXV

F ANTASY S KIRMISH R ULES F OR 1-2 PLAYERS

D ESIGNED F OR S OLO O R C OOPERATIVE PLAY

BY G OTTARDO Z ANCANI

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: 2 CONTENTS • Game Rules • 10 Scenarios • 19 Enemy Sheets – more than 70 Enemies • 2 Character sheets • 6 Table sheets • 2 Campaign Rosters • 1 Campaign Sheet • 32 markers Scale and Measurements All measurements are expressed in Movement Points (MP): when using miniatures in the 15mm-28mm range you can use the equivalence 1MP=1 Inch=2.5 cm, while for smaller miniatures (6-10mm) you can use 1MP=1 cm. In order to use a smaller playing area (or a bigger) you can freely change this value: as an example, for playtesting purposes I use a custom ruler with 1MP=1.5 cm. 1 GAME COMPONENTS 1.1 UNITS The term Unit represents each figure in the game: units under direct control of the player are referred to as Player Units while all the other units, under the control of the game system, are referred to as Enemy Units. Player Characters (also abbreviated with PC) is another synonym for Player Units. Each Unit is described by a series of statistics, presented on a roster like the following: Move: max distance that the Unit can move each turn (expressed in MP). An asterisk after the Move value is a reminder for a special form of movement (ex. Flying). Melee Attack: dice to roll to determine the success of the melee attack. If the value is underlined this unit uses a Special Dice in attack (see 3.1). Melee DMG: damage inflicted by a successful attack (a formula where H: highest die rolled, L: lowest die rolled). Missile Attack and DMG: similar to their melee equivalent stats. Missile Range: the optimal distance for the Missile attack of the Unit. TN/Shield: Target Number. This is the value that you must reach with an attack roll to get a “hit”. If a second value is present after a slash “/”, this value represents the Shield modifier. DR: Damage Reduction. The amount of Damage that is reduced due to armor, agility or other skills. HP: Hit Points. The total amount of damage that a unit can sustain before being eliminated. In addition to the above, Player Units also have the following stats CON: Constitution STR: Strength DEX: Dexterity CON, STR and DEX are used during Skill Checks. The lower part of the roster contains a Damage track where you can keep track of the damage sustained: the modifiers presented here are used in the Advanced Game only (so you can ignore it on your first games). 1.2 SKILL CHECK Some Events and/or special attacks will require a CON, DEX or STR check, defining at the same time the Target Number of the check. Example: STR(11) refers to a STR skill check with TN=11 In order to execute a skill check roll two dice and add the relevant stat: if the number is greater than or equal to the target value then the skill check is successful. The effect of a failure is always described in the special rules requiring the check. Enemies start with CON, DEX and

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: a skill check roll two dice and add the relevant stat: if the number is greater than or equal to the target value then the skill check is successful. The effect of a failure is always described in the special rules requiring the check. Enemies start with CON, DEX and STR =1 unless specified otherwise. 1.3 SCENARIOS Each Scenario Sheet defines the details of the next mission, and in particular the terrain, the objectives and the special rules. In the standard game, you can freely select one of the available Scenarios; in the Campaign Game the next Scenario is determined using the Campaign Sheet (see the Campaign Rules for more details). The map is divided into several “zones”: the map overview provides the overall layout of the zones. On each zone you will place some terrain feature on the basis of the Terrain section instructions. Each Zone should be about 10x10 MP in size, but you can create smaller or larger zones (with a side in the 8-12MP range). Move TN/ Shield DR HP 10 0 11 Melee 2D6 L3 Attack DMG Zombie Missile Attack DMG Range

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: 3 1.4 ENEMIES Enemy forces are described on the Enemy Sheet: in the upper section of those sheets you’ll find the stats of the enemy units, while on the bottom section you’ll find the tables to determine the enemy forces to set in play. 1.5 EVENT MARKERS Event Markers are labeled with a number and are placed face down on the terrain on the basis of the special rules of a scenario. Event Markers represent hidden enemy forces, special events or even red herrings. Event Markers are kept hidden until one Player Unit moves within 2MP of an Event Marker: at this point the Event Marker is revealed and the associated instructions are immediately executed (see the Scenario Sheet). 1.6 ACTIVATION MARKERS Activation markers are used to determine the next Unit to activate; there are three types of markers: • Player • Enemy • End Turn You can replace those markers with colored tokens/cubes: in this case use three colours, one to represent the PC, one to represent the Enemy and the third one to represent the “End Turn”. Activation markers are placed in an opaque cup and are drawn during the game to determine the sequence of activations. 2 STANDARD GAME 2.1 SELECT SCENARIO AND PREPARE THE GAME If this is your first game pick the “Scout” Scenario, otherwise freely select one of the available scenarios or roll 2d6 on the “Random Scenario Selection” table. Prepare the terrain using the instructions found in the “Terrain” section: for each zone of the map you will place one or more terrain features. Follow the instructions in the “Initial Setup” section to Place Event Markers and/or other special features. 2.2 SELECT WARBAND If this is your first game select the first four Units that you find in the Characters Sheet, otherwise select from 3 to 5 Units in the Characters Sheet. Place your Units on the map following the instructions of the “Warband Placement” section in the Scenario sheet. 2.3 SELECT ENEMY Unless the Scenario Sheet specifies otherwise, freely select one Enemy Sheet (or randomly determine it). Each Enemy Sheet defines the Reputation Level required to face this enemy. If the selected Enemy sheet includes an “Enemy Group” section, roll 1d6 and consult the Enemy Group section to determine the enemy group you’ll face. If the scenario requires the placement of Enemy units, proceed following the Scenario instructions. 2.4 TURN SEQUENCE During each turn, you will draw one Activation Marker and execute the activation of the selected Unit (Player or Enemy). You repeat this procedure until all the Units have been activated or the End Turn marker has been drawn for the second time. A new turn then starts, unless the Scenario Objectives have been reached. 2.5 ACTIVATION Place in an opaque cup a number of Activation markers equal to the Units in play, plus the “End Turn” marker. As an example, with three Characters and two Enemies you need to place six markers on the cup: three PC, two Enemies and

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: Objectives have been reached. 2.5 ACTIVATION Place in an opaque cup a number of Activation markers equal to the Units in play, plus the “End Turn” marker. As an example, with three Characters and two Enemies you need to place six markers on the cup: three PC, two Enemies and one End Turn. NPC (per Scenario Rules) also requires one “Player” marker. During the turn, draw a random marker from the cup: the marker label (colour) will determine if you can activate one of your units or instead if you can proceed with the Enemy activation. Place the marker near the unit as a reminder of the completed activation. At the end of each turn, place the markers back in the cup. If a Unit is eliminated from the Scenario, remove one corresponding marker among those already drawn: if no such marker is available remove it from the cup. Similarly, if new Units join the Scenario (for example when new Enemies enter the game or a creature is summoned), place in the cup a number of new markers equal to the number of units joining the fight. 2.5.1 End Turn marker The first time you draw the End Turn marker simply draw another marker and then place the End Turn marker back in the cup.

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: 4 The second time you draw the End Turn marker the turn is over: place all the activation markers back in the cup and start a new turn. It is possible that some Units won’t be activated during one turn due to the End Turn marker: carefully select your Units during the activation! 2.6 PLAYER UNIT ACTIVATION Activated units can Move and then execute one of the possible Actions (very often an attack): in other words, each Unit has two actions available, but the first can only be a Move. 2.6.1 First Action The first action of an activated Unit is always a Move (also referred to as First Move): you can of course skip this action, but you cannot replace it with any other option. 2.6.2 Second Action The second Action of an activated unit can be one of the following: • Move • Quick Attack • Standard Attack (no First Move) • Drop/Pick Up/Exchange equipment • Stand Up • Quaff Potion/Throw Flask/Use Herb • Execute a Special Action 2.6.3 Actions description MOVE A Player Unit can be freely moved in any direction up to its Move stat. See chapter 2.7 for more details about the Movement, but remember that when a Player Unit moves within 2MP of a face down Event Marker, you must immediately reveal it (see 2.9). Exception: Engaged Unit cannot be moved away from the engaging unit(s). STANDARD ATTACK A Player Unit can select this action only if it skipped the First Move. The Unit can execute an Attack against an Enemy (see the Combat rules) without any modifier QUICK ATTACK The Unit can execute an Attack against an Enemy with a +1 TN modifier to the target. This modifier is applied to both Melee and Missile attacks. DROP/PICK UP/EXCHANGE EQUIPMENT The Unit can pick up or drop any single piece of equipment or token. Two adjacent units can Exchange a single piece of equipment or token. QUAFF POTION/THROW FLASK/USE HERB The Unit can use one equipped Item (Potion, Flask, Herb). EXECUTE A SPECIAL ACTION Traits, special equipment or Mission Rules can introduce a Special Action: see the special rules for more details about the effect. 2.7 MOVEMENT Units can be freely moved in any direction up to their Move capability: you cannot move through a terrain labeled as “Impassable” nor can you move through the base of another figure or a terrain feature. A unit moving on a terrain labeled as “difficult” will have its Move stats halved (rounding up). Crossing a linear obstacle costs one additional MP. In order to gain the protection of a terrain feature (see the TERRAIN EFFECTS table), a unit must be adjacent to or inside the feature. 2.7.1 Follow up (Engaging) Once two adversary figures are at a distance of one Movement Point or less, you must place the two Units in base contact (regardless the active unit Move capability); always move the active unit in contact with the stationary unit. 2.7.2 Event Markers When a Player Unit

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: Follow up (Engaging) Once two adversary figures are at a distance of one Movement Point or less, you must place the two Units in base contact (regardless the active unit Move capability); always move the active unit in contact with the stationary unit. 2.7.2 Event Markers When a Player Unit moves within 2MP of a face down Event Marker, you must immediately reveal it: see 2.9 for more details. 2.8 SPECIAL STATUS OF A UNIT 2.8.1 Engaged Units Two adversary units in contact are considered to be Engaged. Engaged units cannot be moved. A single Unit can be Engaged by a maximum of four figures. If a Unit is Engaged by three or four figures, one of the attackers can Disengage as a Special Action: a disengaging unit can be freely moved but cannot engage another figure in the same activation.

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: 5 2.8.2 Prone Units Some special combat actions and/or events can cause a Unit to fall Prone. A Prone Unit must skip its First Move. A Prone Unit can only try to Stand Up with its second action: if the Unit is not Engaged this action is automatic, otherwise the Unit must succeed a DEX check to stand up, with a Target Number equal to 8 plus the number of adjacent enemies. Enemies have a DEX=1 unless specified otherwise. A Prone Unit involved in Combat suffers negative modifiers (See the ATTACK MODIFIERS Table) 2.9 REVEALING EVENT MARKERS When a Player Unit moves within 2MP of a face down Event Marker you must immediately reveal it: lookup the Event Marker # in the Event Markers table in the Scenario sheet and follow the instructions. Only after completing those instructions can the active unit complete its activation (including the current move if still has MP left). One very common action associated to an Event Marker is the one represented by the string “Enemy(A)”: this means that you must now place a new group of enemies on the map (see 2.9.1). 2.9.1 Placing new enemies on the map. The Event Marker action “Enemy (A)” triggers the placement of a new group of enemy units on the map. Proceed as follows • Roll 1d6: if the event was Enemy(A+1) add one to the roll, if the event was Enemy(A-1) subtract one from the roll. • Apply the Warband modifier (from -1 to +2 depending on the size of your warband). (See Tables) • Consult the CLASS A ENCOUNTER table in the Enemy Sheet and lookup for the final result: this will provide the list of enemy units to place on the map. • Place in the Activation cup a number of Enemy markers/token equal to the number of units placed on the map. Place the enemy Units as near as possible to the original Event Marker, at least 1MP from any Player Unit. If the Event Marker was placed inside a terrain feature (ex. a building) try to place the Enemy units inside the same feature. Example: in the current Scenario, the player selected the “Undead” as enemies, and rolled a 1 in the Enemy Group Table; the Player will then face the “Returned” in this Scenario. The Warband in play has 5 characters: this will result on a +1 modifier (see the Warband Modifier table). An Event Marker now calls for an “Enemy(A)” placement: the dice roll is 4, modified to 5 for the Warband size. The entry for “5” in the “Returned” table contains “Zombie (x2), Zombie Leader”, so two Zombies and one Zombie Leader will be placed on the map. 3 COMBAT An Engaged unit with a Melee Attack stat must execute a Melee attack against an adjacent adversary figure. An un-Engaged unit with a Missile attack stat can execute a Missile attack. Both Melee and Missile follow similar rules. 3.1 ATTACK RESOLUTION To determine the result of an attack, roll

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: COMBAT An Engaged unit with a Melee Attack stat must execute a Melee attack against an adjacent adversary figure. An un-Engaged unit with a Missile attack stat can execute a Missile attack. Both Melee and Missile follow similar rules. 3.1 ATTACK RESOLUTION To determine the result of an attack, roll the die indicated by the attacking unit’s “Attack” stat: if the result is greater than or equal to the defender’s TN the attack is successful. Add to this roll any applicable modifier (see Attack Modifiers Table). Some Enemy attacks and PC tactics can require the use of a Special Dice: in such a case, use in the attack roll one dice that’s different from the others (different color, size, material); this dice is referred as the Special Dice. Attacks requiring the use of a Special Dice have an Attack stat underlined (ex. 3D-1): for the Enemies, the effect of the Special Dice is always included in their roster. 3.1.1 Shield A unit with a Shield can add the Shield value to its TN in the following cases: • When attacked by a Missile weapon, always apply the Shield modifier. • When Engaged, a Unit can only apply the Shield modifier to one single attack. Enemy Units will always use the Shield modifier on the first attack. Example: a unit with a “TN/Shield” stat of “9/+2” attacked on by a Short Bow will defend with a TN=9+2=11 3.2 DAMAGE If the attack is successful, determine the damage inflicted using the attacker DMG stat: this is always a formula like “H+L+2” where “H” identifies the value of the highest die rolled in the attack “L” identifies the value of the lowest die rolled in the attack In both cases, if two or more dice are equal you must consider the sum of all the dice with the same value. Example 1. Attack stat is “3D6-1” and DMG is “H+1”. You roll “4”, “5” and “6”: the highest die is 6 then H=6 and DMG=7. If you rolled instead “4”,”5” and “5”, the highest die is 5 but

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: 6 since you’ve got two “5” you must sum both values, so H=5+5=10, and DMG=11. Example 2. Attack stat is “3D6+1” and DMG is “Lx2”. You roll “4”, “5”, “6”: the lowest die is 4 then DMG=Lx2=8. In any case every rolled dice can only be counted once (so if roll a “4” and “4”, a “H+L+1” DMG stat will cause 9 points of damage). Keep track of the DMG points suffered by each unit on its roster: if a unit suffers DMG points equal to its HP the unit is eliminated. 3.3 MISSILE FIRE To execute an attack with a Missile weapon (this applies to every unit with Missile stats), the firing unit must have a clear Line of Sight (LOS) to the target. Simply draw an imaginary line from the center of the firer to the center of the target base: the LOS is blocked if touches • Another Figure • Any Terrain Feature (trees, walls) Buildings always block LOS between two units, except through a door or window. If a clear LOS is available, measure the distance between firer and target. If the distance is greater than the weapon range (as specified in the stats), the attack will incur on a negative modifier (see Attack Modifiers Table). You cannot Missile fire an Engaged Unit. Cover: Units adjacent to a terrain feature gain +2 (light cover) or +4 TN (heavy cover) modifier to Missile attacks. See the Terrain Effects table. 4 ENEMY ACTIVATION To select the Enemy Unit to activate and determine its order roll one dice and consult the ENEMY ACTIVATION table in the current Scenario Sheet. The roll will determine: “ENEMY” column: the unit to activate “ORDER” column: the orders of the activated unit. Each Enemy can be activated only once per turn: place the Activation Marker near the figure as a reminder. 4.1 ENEMY UNIT SELECTION Nearest (target): select the un-engaged Enemy Unit that is nearest to the target. Target can be: another Enemy Unit, a Key Objective or a Player Character. Weakest (target): select the Enemy Unit with the smallest number of HP remaining. Always select damaged units before healthy ones. Engaged (target): select an Enemy unit currently engaged to the target specified. If no unit satisfies the requirements, for example the result states Engaged(Weakest PC) but there are no Engaged units, start from row 1 and proceed downward until you find one entry in the ENEMY ACTIVATION table that can be applied to the current state of the enemy units. If a single Enemy Unit can be activated, roll 1d6 and consult the ENEMY ACTIVATION table considering only the “Order” column. If multiple Enemies could be activated, give priority to characters nearest to a Key Objective (if any) or those marked as “Leader” or “Champion/Hero”. 4.2 ENEMY ORDERS Design Note: this section is the one that most requires the use of “common sense”: evaluate the enemy orders as a guideline for the unit actions, trying to leverage at max the terrain features in

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: a Key Objective (if any) or those marked as “Leader” or “Champion/Hero”. 4.2 ENEMY ORDERS Design Note: this section is the one that most requires the use of “common sense”: evaluate the enemy orders as a guideline for the unit actions, trying to leverage at max the terrain features in terms of cover. Attack (target). Order interpretation: the Enemy unit will move toward the target in order to attack it. Units with Missile and/or Magic capabilities will try to avoid entering in Melee. If the unit has a Missile or Magic capability, stop the unit when he’s in range of the target with a clear LOS and execute a Missile attack. When possible, place the active enemy adjacent to a terrain feature that provides cover. Only if all the player units are Engaged a Missile Unit will attack using its Melee weapon. If the unit has only Melee capabilities, move the unit to engage the target and then attack it. If the unit is Engaged, resolve the Melee attack. If the target is a terrain feature (ex. a building), the unit will try to attack any Player unit inside or adjacent to the location. If no Player units are guarding the location, the unit will move adjacent to it and will keep control of it. Support (target). Order interpretation: the Enemy unit will try to help the target, engaging the player units in contact with the target or firing to the units around the target. The active Enemy unit will try to help the target (enemy) unit: • If the active unit has a Missile stat, then it will try to move in order to gain a clear LOS to the nearest, not engaged player unit nearest to the target; if

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: 7 this is possible the active unit will then execute its attack against it. • If the active unit has a Melee stat, then it will try to engage the nearest player unit engaged with the target. If the target is not engaged then the active unit will try to engage the player unit nearest to the target. • The supporting unit will always try to end its move nearer to the target. Hold (target). Order interpretation: the Enemy unit will try to keep control of the target, moving toward it if needed and attacking any enemy in or adjacent to the target (in general “menacing” the target). The active Enemy unit will try to keep control of the target (often a terrain feature): • If the active Enemy unit is not adjacent to the target, move it trying to reach the target as fast as possible. • If the active unit has a Missile stat it will attack the easiest player unit in LOS (if any) • If the active unit has a Melee stat then it will attack any player unit in or adjacent to the target; if such player unit is not present the unit will keep its position. 4.3 COVER Enemy units will try to maximize the presence of terrain features providing cover, like trees or boulders. If an activated enemy doesn’t engage a PC, place the enemy adjacent to a terrain providing cover if such a feature is available in the direction where the unit is moving. Design note: an enemy unit will always try to move closer to its target but will not “blindly” move on a straight line when a terrain feature providing cover is available, even if this means diverting a little from the shortest path. 5 CAMPAIGN GAME In the Campaign Game, you create your own Warband from the very beginning of its existence: each Scenario will make possible to improve your Characters, gain Loot and Reputation to become the greatest Warband. 5.1 WARBAND CREATION At the beginning of a new Campaign, the first step is the creation your Warband: you will need to create five characters, including the Leader of the Warband. The character creation process is described in the next paragraphs: simply generate the Leader and the four characters, keeping track of their stats in the Campaign Roster. 5.1.1 Creation of a new character Give your new Character a Name; each new Character will start with the same stats: Move: 5 TN: 7 HP: 8 STR=1 CON=1 DEX=1 DR=0 Each new Character has 1 Experience Point (XP) available, while the Leader has 4 XPs. You can now spend the XP on the Character Advancement Table to improve your character (see 5.1.3). Keep track of the stats using the Character Roster. 5.1.2 Weapons and Armor selection You can now assign one or two weapons to your new Character: if you select two Weapons, you must pick one Melee and one Missile weapon. Please note that some weapons require a specific

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: (see 5.1.3). Keep track of the stats using the Character Roster. 5.1.2 Weapons and Armor selection You can now assign one or two weapons to your new Character: if you select two Weapons, you must pick one Melee and one Missile weapon. Please note that some weapons require a specific Trait to be used by the character. Select the weapons in the Weapons Table and copy the relevant data to the Character Roster. Assign the Character one piece of Armor from the ARMOR TABLE: this will impact the Character TN and possibly the DR. You can also select one shield but consider that if the character is wearing a two-Handed weapon, it will not be able to use it anyway. 5.1.3 Character Advancement (XP) Each character involved in a Scenario will gain XP at the end of the Scenario (see 5.11.1). XP are spent to increase the stats (example: +1 to Melee) or to gain specific Traits (unique abilities). The CHARACTER ADVANCEMENT table describes all the available advancements: each improvement has a cost (in XP) and a max number of times that you can select it for the same character. If multiple costs are specified (ex. “3/6”), the different value represents the incremental cost of the advancement. Example: an advancement with “3/6” value will cost 3XP for the first selection and 6XP for the second selection. Keep track of the XP available for each character on the Character roster. One important note about the two different attack improvements (apply to both Missile and Melee): • a “+1” improvement adds one to the attack formula regardless the weapon selected (e.g. a “2D” DMG became “2D+1”). This improvement can be selected multiple times. • a “+1D” improvement adds one dice to the attack formula of a specific weapon. (e.g. a “2D+1” DMG became “2D+1+1D”, equivalent to “3D+1”). This improvement can only be selected once per weapon.

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: 8 5.2 REPUTATION AND LOOT Reputation of your Warband is measured by its Reputation Level (RL); RL starts at 1 at the beginning of a new Campaign and can be increased by spending a number of Reputation Points equal to two times the next level (for example, to reach RL 3 you need 3x2=6 RP). Reputation Points (RP) are gained by completing Scenario objectives, and in particular by taking part in the Pit fights (see “The Pit” scenario). 5.2.1 Loot Loot represents the riches found after winning a Scenario: the “Search” Scenarios are the main method to gaining Loot. Loot points can be spent in several ways: to improve your characters, to increase the Reputation of your warband, to gain special equipment. See the LOOT POINTS section in the Campaign Tables for the details. 5.2.2 Warband Starting Stats Your Warband will start with Reputation Level=1, RP=0 and Loot=0. Mark those values in the Campaign Roster. 5.3 CAMPAIGN TURN A single turn in the Campaign Game represents about one week of time: each turn follows this sequence: • Recovery • Character Advancement • Recruitment • Scenario selection • Warband Preparation • Start Scenario • End Scenario Briefing 5.4 RECOVERY PHASE In this phase, you must check for the recovery of the Characters wounded in the previous battle. 5.4.1 Recovering DMG Characters with less than half of their max HP will recover 1D6+CON DMG points, while the other characters will fully recover. 5.5 CHARACTER ADVANCEMENT You can now spend the available XPs to improve your characters: see 5.1.3 for additional details. 5.6 RECRUITMENT Roll 2d6 and add the Reputation Level of your Warband, then consult the RECRUITMENT table to verify if a new adventurer reached your camp. If a new Character is available, you can proceed with Character creation procedure (see 5.1.1) 5.7 SCENARIO SELECTION In the Campaign Game you will use the Campaign Sheet to determine the next Scenario. The Campaign Sheet includes a flowchart with the name of the available Scenarios. You always start a new Campaign on the “Scout” box in the center: keep track of your current position in the flowchart with a counter (or a pencil mark). To determine the next Scenario roll 1d6 and look at the arrows moving out of your current position in the Campaign Sheet; each arrow is labeled with a value (or range of values): follow the arrow whose label corresponds to the dice roll to determine your next Scenario (and mark the new position in the flowchart). Example: You are on the “Scout” box and you roll a “4”: you must move to the “Attack” Scenario box. Some arrows are labeled with a value followed by an asterisk: if this is the case, you can always select this Scenario as the next one instead of rolling the die. Example: you can always move to the “Search” Scenario box from the “Scout” box. This represents the result of your scouting activities. If one or more of your Characters have been Captured, you

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: the case, you can always select this Scenario as the next one instead of rolling the die. Example: you can always move to the “Search” Scenario box from the “Scout” box. This represents the result of your scouting activities. If one or more of your Characters have been Captured, you can select the Scenario “Free Prisoners” as the next Scenario regardless your position in the Campaign Sheet. 5.7.1 Challenges Challenges are unique missions for Blood on the Blade, scenarios that you normally will be able to play only once during your Campaign. Challenges represent special encounters, areas discovered during your exploration of Aghamon, unique contracts stipulated by the Warband leader, singular adversaries: due to their uniqueness those missions cannot be repeated. There are two main types of Challenges: Loot and Reputation: a Loot Challenge will provide special Loot for your Warband while a Reputation Challenge… well you can easily guess. During a Campaign, you can introduce a Challenge after any standard Scenario: in the Scenario Selection phase do not roll to determine the next Scenario, but instead play the selected Challenge. After the Challenge, the Campaign reverts to the standard rules. Additional Challenges can be found in the Aghamon Gazettes, the free expansion volumes for Blood on the Blade.

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: 9 5.8 WARBAND PREPARATION You can now select from 3 to 6 characters that will take place to the next Scenario. Any non-Captured character can be selected. 5.8.1 Equipping characters You can now (re)assign weapons and armor to the selected characters (see 5.1.2) if needed. Special Equipment (see 8.6) can also be selected in this phase. 5.8.2 Captured characters Some events can cause the capture of one Character: mark the character as “Captured” on the Campaign Roster as a reminder. Captured Characters cannot take part in any Warband action; a Captured character can be freed by performing one of the following actions: • By paying 1 Loot as a ransom • Successfully completing the “Free Prisoners” Scenario 5.9 START SCENARIO The Standard rules for preparing the terrain apply also on the Campaign game. 5.10 ESCAPE In the Campaign Game, you can escape from a Scenario (except “The Pit”) by moving your Units to the Entry side of the table. The Scenario is considered lost but you can save the life of you Characters. Escaping a Scenario has clearly an impact on your Reputation: you always lose one RP and you cannot gain any RP associated with the Scenario (and any Loot token is considered lost). 5.11 END SCENARIO BRIEFING At the end of a scenario you must check for the Rewards and the fate of the Eliminated Characters. 5.11.1 Victory Points Once you complete a Scenario, you can gain the associated Rewards, normally in terms of Victory Points (VP), Reputation Points and Loot (exception: Escape, see 5.10) VPs can be spent as follows: • Spend one VP to gain 2 XP • Spend two VP to gain 1 RP Keep track of the RP, XP and Loot in the Campaign Roster. 5.11.2 Escape Effects If you’ve Escaped from the Scenario, roll on the ESCAPE TABLE and determine the effects. 5.11.3 Eliminated units Eliminated Characters still have the option to survive to the battle wounds: those that will make it will in any case suffer a permanent injury. For each player unit eliminated during the Scenario roll a d66 and consult the ELIMINATED CHARACTERS table: if a character is not removed from the Campaign Roster, mark the permanent injury that he will suffer (if any) and set its DMG to HP-2 (i.e. the character has only two HP left). In addition, any character whose STR, DEX or CON has been reduced to a negative value during the last scenario is also removed by the Campaign Roster unless you spend enough XP to restore this attribute to zero or more. 6 MAGIC Some units are capable of controlling the arcane powers of the nine magic channels: the special rules to deal with Magic are described in this chapter. Characters capable of controlling the magic channels (also referred as “magic users”) have two additional stats: POW: Power. This represents both the strength of the magic users in controlling the magic and its rank. Mana: This represents the amount of “magic energy” available to

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: Magic are described in this chapter. Characters capable of controlling the magic channels (also referred as “magic users”) have two additional stats: POW: Power. This represents both the strength of the magic users in controlling the magic and its rank. Mana: This represents the amount of “magic energy” available to the character during one Scenario. Magic users start with POW=1; the base Mana available is equal to 2xPOW (Mana can also increase by spending XP in the Character Advancement phase, see 5.1.3). To keep track of those stats and of the additional special rules applying to the magic users, a different character roster labeled “Magic User” is available in the Campaign Roster. Magic School: each Magic User must be associated to one of the three major schools of Magic: Life, Death and Balance (this will influence the available spells). 6.1 MAGIC USERS SPECIAL RULES Magic channels are strongly influenced by metal: for this reason, magic users cannot use anything metallic. Magic users are treated as standard units with the following exceptions • A new magic user starts the game with 2XP instead of 1XP (see 5.1.1). • Magic users can only use Leather armor (no shields)

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: 10 • Magic users can only use non-metallic melee weapons (Clubs/Staff) • Magic users have two additional special Actions available: CAST SPELL and REST. 6.2 SPELLS Spells are found in the Spell Table section. A magic user can memorize a number of spells equal to 3x its POW: each spell has the following attributes Name: name of the spell. Level: the rank of the spell. Difficulty: the complexity Mana: the cost in Mana to cast the spell Effect: the effect of the spell School: the school of Magic associated to the spell. A Magic user can only select a spell whose Level is equal to or less than its POW. In addition, if the Spell belongs to the Life or Death Schools of Magic, a magic user can only select it if he belongs to the same school. See the SPELLS TABLE for the complete list of the available spells. 6.3 CASTING A SPELL CAST SPELL is a Special Action only available to the magic users. In order to cast a spell a magic user must skip its First move (to be able to concentrate) and then spend its Second Action to cast the spell: the magic user can only attempt to cast a spell if the remaining Mana equal to or greater than the Mana cost of the Spell. The target of the spell must be visible and within 10 MP. To cast a spell roll 2d6 and add the POW of the magic user: if the result is equal or greater than the spell Difficulty the cast is successful otherwise the attempt failed. Successful spell: spend the require Mana (keep track of the Mana in the Character roster) and apply the Spell effect. Unless specified otherwise, the spell effects last one single turn. Failed attempt: spend 1 Mana. 6.3.1 Enemy Spellcasters Enemies capable of casting spells have a “Magic” entry in their roster that contains the die to roll, the spell Difficulty and the max range of the spell. In the above example, the Enemy will be able to successfully cast a spell on a result of 9 or more when rolling 2D+2. The effect of the spell is also included in the Enemy roster. 6.4 POWER SURGE The Power Surge allows a magic user to trade part of his health to successfully cast a spell. After rolling the dice to cast a spell, a magic user can spend one HP to add a +1 modifier to the roll. There’s no limit at the number of HPs that can be spent for a Power Surge: each HP spent will add a +1 to the roll. 6.5 SUMMONING SPELLS A Summoning is a spell that places on the game a new Unit (normally an Undead): when a Magic User successfully cast a summoning, place the summoned Unit(s) within 1MP from the Magic User and then place a new Activation Marker(s) in the activation cup (Player or Enemy depending on the Magic User's faction). If a Magic User that previously

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: a new Unit (normally an Undead): when a Magic User successfully cast a summoning, place the summoned Unit(s) within 1MP from the Magic User and then place a new Activation Marker(s) in the activation cup (Player or Enemy depending on the Magic User's faction). If a Magic User that previously summoned one or more creatures is killed, immediately remove the summoned units from the game (also remove the corresponding Activation Markers following the standard Activation rules). 6.6 MANA RECOVERY (REST) The REST Special action allows a magic user to recover one Mana Point. 6.7 MAGIC RESISTANCE Some units are capable of resisting to the effect of magic against them: the Magic Resistance reflects this capability. Some units (and some equipment) have the special stat MR=Magic Resistance. The MR is added to the Difficulty of a spell targeting the unit. 7 TABLE SETUP Detailed terrain placement has been designed to be left to the imagination of the player, especially considering that this is a solitaire game. In this chapter, some guidelines are provided to setup the table for those that prefer a more “structured” approach. 7.1 ZONES Each Zone should have a size of about 10x10 MP: this means that with 28mm figures you should consider a 10x10”

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: 11 square for each Zone. For bigger or smaller games, you can consider Zones with a side in the 8-12MP range. As a guideline, at least 30% of a Zone should be occupied by some type of Terrain: if needed roll again on the Terrain table of the Scenario to generate additional features to reach the 30% of coverage. 7.2 TERRAIN Single Terrain features like Buildings or Boulders should be placed together in order to clearly create a section of the Zone. Difficult terrain, like mud, should cover at least 30% of the Zone: this type of terrain can be represented by a single piece or by multiple pieces combined. Linear obstacles like walls should be placed to separate the different sections of a Zone: for example, separating the Buildings section from the Trees section. 7.2.1 Trees The term “tree” doesn’t necessarily indicate a single plant, but rather an “area with a few trees”. This area should be at least 2x2 MP in size but can be larger (up to 2x) especially if this is the only terrain feature on a zone. This means for example that if a Zone has one single “tree” on it you should create an area of 3-4 MP in size. 7.2.2 Using existing terrain You can of course use any existing terrain models or even printed terrain maps to play the game: in such a case simply divide the terrain area on equally sized zones (normally six) depending the Scenario, then place the Event markers following the Scenario rules (normally near a terrain feature). Again, never use Terrain rules too strictly: if you want to create your own map using your preferred models (like for example a cemetery, an ancient temple) feel free to do it. Place any element you want on the map then distribute the Event Markers following the Scenario rules. 8 ADVANCED RULES These rules can be included to the game to add some additional details with a small price in increased complexity. Each rule can be added individually to the game. 8.1 DAMAGE TRACK The lower part of a Unit roster contains a Damage Track where you can keep track of the damage sustained by each Unit. The Damage Track is divided in two or three zones by vertical lines (roughly corresponding to “healthy”, “wounded” and “seriously wounded”): under each zone there’s a list of modifiers that you need to apply when the Unit damage reaches the corresponding area. Start marking the wounds from right to left, marking the first available circle in the current zone before moving on the next one. Example 1: two different units, one per row. Unit #1 (with 5 HP of damage) suffers a -1 Atk modifier, while Unit #2 (8 HP of damage) suffers -2 Atk, -1 TN and -1 Move modifers. Example 2: one unit, with zones spanning multiple rows, suffers 9 HP of damage. The circles are marked from right to left, filling the rightmost zone before proceeding to the next one.

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: Atk modifier, while Unit #2 (8 HP of damage) suffers -2 Atk, -1 TN and -1 Move modifers. Example 2: one unit, with zones spanning multiple rows, suffers 9 HP of damage. The circles are marked from right to left, filling the rightmost zone before proceeding to the next one. Possible modifiers are: -X Atk: apply a -X modifier to any attack roll +X Atk: apply a +X modifier to any attack roll -X TN: reduce the TN stat of X points -X Move: reduce the Move stat of X points -X Check: apply a -X modifier to any Skill Check. This DRM also apply to CAST SPELL actions. 8.1.1 Damage Track for Player Characters A Player Character’s Damage Track is divided in three zones: 1..2 HP (Seriously wounded). Modifiers -2 Atk, -1 Check, -1 TN, -1 Move 3..half HP (rounded down). Modifiers -1 Atk, -1 Check half HP..max HP. No modifiers The first zone is already present in the Character roster: when you create a new Character (see 5.1.1) or update its HP stat you must trace in the Damage Track a vertical line corresponding to half (rounded down) of the HP. Example: for a Character with 11 HP you need to trace a line after the 5th box (11/2=5.5, rounded down 5) #1 #2 ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ -2 Atk -1 TN -1 Move -1 Atk XXXXX X X X X X X X X #1 ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ -2 Atk -1 TN -1 Move -1 Atk ○ ○ ○ XXXXXX X X X -2 Atk -1 Check -1 TN -1 Move ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ 10 -1 Atk -1 Check 5 15

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: 12 8.2 MOUNTED UNITS Mounted Units require some extra rules to represent their capabilities and uniqueness but are formidable actors on the battlefield: I’ve decided to keep the mount separated from the rider to allow a richer set of gaming options. Mounted Units are described with two sets of attributes: the top section describes the rider, while the mount is described on the bottom. In addition to the standard traits, Mounts have an additional trait: Ride: this value represents the difficulty in controlling the mount. The Damage Track of a mounted Unit includes an additional area representing the wounds of the mount, with grey solid circles used to represent the damage points. At the very bottom of the mounted Unit roster there’s a small table used to determine how to distribute the damage inflicted to this unit. 8.2.1 Ride Check In some occasions, a Ride check will be required to test the control of the mount by the rider: in this case roll 2d6 and add any Riding Skill bonus to the result (Riding Skill can be acquired as Character Advancements). If the result is equal or greater than the mount RIDE attribute the check is successful. 8.2.2 Movement Mounted Units always use the Move value of the mount until dismounted. A Mounted Unit can always try to disengage from a non- mounted unit: execute a Ride check, if successful you can immediately disengage the unit. Enemies will use this capability only if specified by the special rules in the enemy roster. 8.2.3 Combat Mounted units use their Melee and/or Missile attributes in combat: when attacking a non-Mounted target, a Mounted unit gains a bonus of +1 to its Attack value. This bonus is not applied when the target is wielding a weapon with the “REACH” trait (see also 8.7). 8.2.4 Charge If a mounted PC moves straight at least 4MP to engage an Enemy, he gains a +2 DRM modifier for the first attack (in addition to the standard +1 modifier for mounted attacker). Charge bonus are negated by targets wielding weapons with the “REACH” trait 8.2.5 Mount Attacks A Rider can try to order its mount to attack instead of performing a standard Attack; execute a Ride check: if the check is successful then execute a Standard Attack with the Mount Melee attributes (If the check is not successful the rider cannot execute a standard attack). Some Mounts are specifically trained for the combat and can perform an attack on a specific result of the Special Dice: see the Enemy Unit roster or the Mount roster for the details. The Mount attack in this case is performed without the need of a Ride check and in addition to the attack of the rider. 8.2.6 Damage When a mounted Unit is attacked, roll 1d6 and consult the table on the bottom of the Unit roster to determine who will receive the attack (rider or mount): on the basis of this dice roll use the raider or the mount TN

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: the attack of the rider. 8.2.6 Damage When a mounted Unit is attacked, roll 1d6 and consult the table on the bottom of the Unit roster to determine who will receive the attack (rider or mount): on the basis of this dice roll use the raider or the mount TN to determine the effect of the attack then mark the damage (if any) on the corresponding damage track. Example: an Archer (Attack: 2D, Damage L) is targeting a Mounted Swordsman. One dice is rolled to determine who will receive the arrow. The Mounted Swordsman roster includes the following table ⚫ ⚫ Move TN/ Shield DR HP 9 -1 9 3D-1 H5* Attack DMG Mounted Archer Attack DMG Range #1 Sabre Leather Armor -2 Atk -1 TN -1 Move (-2 Move) -1 Atk (-1 Move) Move 9 Attack 3D DMG H Horse TN 8 DR Rider (1-4) Mount (5-6) Ride 7 ○ ○ ○ ○ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫⚫ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ *When Dismounted ⚫ ⚫ #2 ○ ○ ○ ○ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫ ⚫⚫ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ Charge. If a Mounted Swordsman moves straight at least 4MP to engage a PC, he gains a +2 Atk modifier for the first attack (in addition to the standard +1 modifier for mounted attacker) mount mount Melee Missile Melee Rider (1-4) Mount (5-6) Rider (1-4) Mount (5-6)

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: 13 The dice roll is “5”, hence the arrow will hit the Mount. The Archer rolls two dices (Attack: 2D) and obtains a ‘6’ and a ‘3’: since the TN of the Mount is 8 the attack is successful and inflicts 3 hits to the Mount (no DR) that are marked on the Damage Track. 8.2.7 Dismounting A PC can Dismount from its mount by performing a Ride check as a Special Action: add +2 to check if the PC skipped its First Move. If the check is successful, the PC is now Dismounted and will be treated as such for the rest of the scenario (the mount is considered to be leaving the area). If the PC fails the check, is still considered Dismounted but in addition he suffers 1d3 points of damage (1d6 if wearing a full or half Plate) with no DR. 8.2.8 Killed Mount When a mount is killed, the unit mounting it must immediately execute a Ride check or suffer 1d3 points of damage (1d6 if wearing a full or half Plate). The unit is then considered Dismounted. Enemies Dismounted due to the killing of their mount never execute a check. 8.2.9 Killed Rider When a Mounted Unit is Killed, immediately remove the miniature from the game. 8.2.10 PC using a mount In order to use any Mount, a PC must gain the “Riding Experience” Character Advancement. The available mounts are described in the Mount Sheet, where you can also find the cost to acquire the Mount. The “Riding Skill” bonuses can also be acquired as Character Advancements. 8.2.11 Weapon and Armor restrictions When using a Mount, a PC cannot use any two-handed weapon, with the exception of the Spear. Only a Small Shield can be equipped while mounted; the use of (Half) Plates is only possible with some specific Mount types: see the Mount Sheet for the details. 8.2.12 Optional Rule: Falling When you’ve completely filled one section of the Damage Track of a mount, immediately make a Ride check. If the check is successful, the Unit was able to control the mount, otherwise the Unit is immediately Dismounted and suffers 1d3 points of damage (1d6 if wearing a full or half Plate). When using this rule, Enemy units are always considered having a Riding Skill of zero. 8.2.13 Mount in the Campaign Game In the Recovery Phase of the Campaign Game roll 1d6 and consult the MOUNT RECOVERY TABLE, adding +1 to the roll if the Mount has three or less HP remaining. 8.3 EVENT MARKERS ACTIVATION Event Markers are kept static in the Standard Game: the following rules make possible to activate the EM during a Scenario, add more gaming options and increasing the “Fog of War” effect. Some scenarios include a “Event Marker Activation” box where are listed the EM that can be activated during the game. Only the EM included in this box can be activated: the other EM are kept as static. When using the EM activation rules, place

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: increasing the “Fog of War” effect. Some scenarios include a “Event Marker Activation” box where are listed the EM that can be activated during the game. Only the EM included in this box can be activated: the other EM are kept as static. When using the EM activation rules, place two Enemy Markers in the Activation Pool at the beginning of the Scenario. When an Enemy Marker is drawn (and there are no Enemies on the Map) roll 1d6 on the EVENT MARKERS ACTIVATION TABLE to determine the EM to activate, then roll again to determine its order. You find the EVENT MARKERS ACTIVATION TABLE in the Tables document. The EM will always be selected referring to a PC (nearest, farthest): this PC will be referred as the “Target” in the EM activation Orders. Even if in LOS of a PC, activated Event Markers are automatically revealed only when they engage a PC: a PC will reveal an activated EM in LOS and within 10 MP during its activation (static EM are revealed only when a Player Unit moves within 2MP of distance). When an EM is revealed, and one or more Enemy characters enter the game, remove the two initial Enemy Markers from the Activation pool. If at the end of a game turn there are no Enemies on the Map but there are still Event Markers, placed two Enemy Markers in the Activation pool and proceed again with an EM activation when an Enemy Marker is drawn. 8.4 FLYING UNITS A Unit with a “[Flying]” trait in its roster is considered a Flying Units (the Move value has an asterisk as a reminder). Flying Units ignore obstacles while moving but cannot stop the movement on an impassable terrain. Flying Units have two TNs: the first is used when flying while the second, between parenthesis, is used when Engaged. Unengaged flying Units are considered as flying and cannot be attacked with Melee weapons (use the first TN of the

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: 14 Unit for the Missile attacks against it). A PC cannot engage a Flying Unit. When Engaged, a flying Unit can be attacked in Melee, using the TN between parenthesis. 8.5 SWAPPING WEAPON Instead of “freely” using any available weapon, you can use the following Action to manage to action of swapping a weapon (from Missile to Melee and vice versa, or to swap between two different Melee weapons) SWAP WEAPON • A Player Unit can select this action only if he skips the First Move. This action makes possible to exchange the current weapon from Melee to Missile or vice versa: mark on the Character roster the equipped weapon). Note: Enemy units can “freely” use both weapons; this is one of the few balance mechanisms for the Enemy forces. 8.6 SPECIAL EQUIPMENT In the Warband Preparation phase, you can purchase the items included in the Equipment section of the Campaign Tables: some items are always free, some have a fixed cost (in Loot Points) while others are freely available if, rolling one dice, you obtain a specific result. Example: an Explosive Flask is available if you roll a 5 or more You always only roll once per Scenario, regardless the number of characters in your warband. Some Items allows a PC to execute an Attack against an Enemy: those are considered as Special Attacks and not as Missile Attacks even if the item is tossed again the enemy. 8.6.1 Equipment Limits A character can only equip two “Items” and one “Mount” in addition to the standard weapons and armor. 8.6.2 Explosive Flask A PC can throw an Explosive Flask to a target within 4MP of distance. The attack roll is 2D+1 and the DMG caused is H+2: the explosion effects all the units (including friendly ones) within 1MP of range: you can of course target a spot in the map instead of a unit to maximize this area effect. 8.6.3 Acid Flask A PC can throw an Acid Flask to a single target within 4MP of distance. The attack roll is 2D+2 and the DMG caused is H (ignoring DR): in addition, the target permanently loses one point of TN. 8.7 SPECIAL WEAPONS TRAITS PENETRATION(X) A weapon with a PENETRATION(X) diminishes the Damage Reduction of the target armor by X. Since the DR is a negative value, you must add the Penetration value X to the DR to get the modified value • Example: a unit equipped with a Morningstar with Penetration(1) hits a target with a Chain Mail (DR -3). The DR is reduced to -2 (-3 DR +1 Penetration = -2) POISON(TN, DMG) Poison damage is described with two values: TN and DMG; example: Poison(8, 1D) is a Poison damage with TN=8 and DMG=1D. Any Unit damaged by a Poison attack, in addition to the standard damage must immediately execute a CON(TN) check. If this check is failed, then the Unit suffers the damage specified. Some Poison can cause a special type of damage (like paralysis): in

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: a Poison damage with TN=8 and DMG=1D. Any Unit damaged by a Poison attack, in addition to the standard damage must immediately execute a CON(TN) check. If this check is failed, then the Unit suffers the damage specified. Some Poison can cause a special type of damage (like paralysis): in such a case the attacking Unit roster will include the details to deal with the special damage. REACH. A unit wielding a weapon with the REACH Trait follows the special rules: • The equipped weapon negates every type of CHARGE bonus of an adversary, unless the unit is already Engaged. • The TN of the unit is increased by 2 (+2TN) in the turn when an adversary engages it (unless the unit is already Engaged). SINGLE USE A weapon with a SINGLE USE trait can only be used once per Scenario.

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: 15 8.8 SPECIAL ENEMY TRAITS FEAR(X) The first time a PC tries to engage an Enemy with a Fear(X) trait, roll 2d6: if the result is greater or equal to X, the PC can engage the Enemy, otherwise it must spend the remaining MPs to move as far away as possible from the Enemy, ending its activation. If already engaged, the first time a PC tries to attack an Enemy with a Fear(X) trait roll 2d6: if the result is greater or equal to X the PC can attack the Enemy, otherwise it loses the current attack. Repeat this check on the next attacks until the PC is successful. Once passed, the Fear test is considered valid for the rest of the scenario against similar Enemies. The FEARLESS Character advancement can provide a bonus to the Fear test. 8.9 COMPANIONS Companions are special characters added to your warband. Each companion is described by a Companion Sheet. Each companion has a cost to be purchased and some specific requirements (normally in terms of special Skills that one Character need to have). A newly purchased companion must be assigned to a single Character satisfying alle the prerequisites; this PC is designated as the Master of the companion. Companions never count for your warband size but only one type of companion can be selected when starting a scenario. In the initial setup of a scenario, place the companion within one MP from its Master. 8.9.1 Companion Activation Add one special activation marker for the Companion to the activation pool (you can use the Player marker with a dark background). Once activated, a companion will: • Follow the last order received (see below) • Defend his Master if no other order has been given. The companion will move in order to stay within one MP from his Master. If an Enemy engages the Master, the Companion will attack this Enemy. 8.9.2 Giving orders to a Companion As a Special Action, a Master can give its Companion one of the available orders. Each Companion Sheet has a specific section containing the available orders: see this section for the additional details. 8.9.3 Dead or unconscious Master If the Master is dead or unconscious, the companion, unless Engaged, will move to reach the position of the Master body and then will stay here; the companion will attach any character (including a PC) that moves within 1MP from the Master body. If attacked, the companion will attack the character that attacked it. At the end of the Scenario, a new character satisfying the prerequisites can be assigned as a new Master to the companion spending one additional XP. 8.9.4 Companion XP Companion gain XP as the other characters; XP can be spent to increase its stats: see the Companion Advancement Table in the Companion Sheet for the available advancement. 8.9.5 Recovery Phase Unless specified otherwise, companions fully recover their wounds in the Recovery Phase. 8.10 NIGHT RULES (OPTIONAL) Night Rules can be required by specific Scenarios or can

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text: XP can be spent to increase its stats: see the Companion Advancement Table in the Companion Sheet for the available advancement. 8.9.5 Recovery Phase Unless specified otherwise, companions fully recover their wounds in the Recovery Phase. 8.10 NIGHT RULES (OPTIONAL) Night Rules can be required by specific Scenarios or can instead be selected in other Scenarios to add some additional challenges to the game. If a Scenario includes a “Night Mission” box, you’ll find here the procedure to check if the Scenario must be played during the Night. This check is always optional. During a Night Scenario, the line of sight is limited to 6 MP for every Human/Humanoid character: non-human Enemies are not limited to 6MP of visibility unless specified otherwise. Ambush: when a new Enemy is placed on the map (for example as a result of an Event Marker), roll 1d6 for each Enemy figure: (1-5) place the Enemy marker/token in the Activation cup (6) the Enemy is immediately activated; place the Enemy marker/token near the Enemy figure after the activation A scenario played with the Night Rules will provide your Warband 1d6 additional XPs at the end of the game. 8.11 RAIN (OPTIONAL) Those rules can be added to any scenario to add more variety and create new gaming options. At the beginning of a new Mission roll 1d6: on a result of (1- 5) weather is Clear, while on a result of (6) weather is Rain.

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook
text:
16

If the starting weather is Rain, then you will need to check

at the end of each turn if the weather is changing: roll 1d6

and consult the following table

Weather Roll

(1) Weather is Clear

(2-5) Weather is Rain

(6) Weather is Heavy Rain

Weather Effects

Clear:

no effects.

-1 to the next Weather Roll

Rain:

-1 MP to human characters

-1 DRM to Ranged attacks

Heavy Rain:

-2 MP to human characters

-1 MP to non-human character

-2 DRM to Ranged attacks

Visibility limited to 8 MP

+1 to the next Weather Roll

9 GAME CONVENTIONS AND

ABBREVIATIONS

1d6: roll one standard six-sided die.

1d66: roll of two six-sided dice distinguishable from each

other by colour or size. The first die represents the tens

digit, and the second die the ones digit.

Atk: attack value of a unit.

DMG: Damage. The amount of damage inflicted by an

attack. Also used to refer to the total damage sustained by

one unit.

DR: Damage Reduction. The amount of Damage that is

reduced due to armour, agility or other skills.

DRM: Dice Roll Modifier. A number (positive or negative)

to be added to the dice roll.

EM: Event Marker. A brown marker with a question mark

on one side and a white number on the other side

HP: Hit Points. The total amount of damage that a unit can

sustain before being eliminated.

MP: Movement Point

PC: Player Character. A miniature directly controlled by

the player.

TN: Target Number. This is the value that you must reach

with a roll to get a success. TN are used to determine the

result of an attack or a skill check.

subject: Boardgames & Role-Playing Games/Blood on the Blade/Core Rulebook

17

A NOTE ABOUT THE LANGUAGE

English is not my native language, so typos and

grammar errors are expected in the rules and in the

game components.

If you notice such an error, please send a note to my

email zak965@libero.it. Everybody contributing in such

a “spell-checking” exercise will receive an exclusive

Enemy Sheet (i.e. something that will never be

available in the published content or on the online

support site).

Updated (fixed) versions of the game components will

be released on the Wargame Vault site.

Thanks in advance!
